protocol: environmental - heat exposure/heat exhaustion environmental - heat stroke (protocol 5 - 2)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 for a 65-year-old female experiencing fainting or near-fainting at [CITY] address.
Hello, I'm Ryan, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 today?
She was having trouble walking, had to sit on the sidewalk and seemed very weak.
Can you tell me what year it is, ma'am?
Good. Do you know where you are?
I'm in [CITY] somewhere.
Could you open your eyes again if I clap my hands?
Yes, I can do that.
Repeat after me: "I see clowns at the circus."
I see clowns at the circus.
Squeeze my hand when I count one, two, three, please.
I squeezed it already.
Your airway is clear, Jane. Let's check your breathing now.
Respirations are 18, shallow but not in distress per visual assessment.
Are you having to work hard to breathe?
Not really, but I feel something wrong in my chest.
Pulse is strong and fast at 132 bpm, and BP reads 154/82.
Heart rate is 132 beats per minute.
Respirations are 18 per minute, shallow but consistent.
Blood pressure is 154 over 82 mm Hg.
Blood glucose is 131 mg/dL.
Pulse oximetry readings are at 96% saturation.
EKG is showing sinus tachycardia at the moment.
Jane, do you have any chronic conditions?
I have high blood pressure and type 2 diabetes.
Are you currently on any medications for that?
No, I ran out and haven't refilled them.
Are you allergic to any medications?
No known drug allergies.
When was the last thing you ate or drank?
A couple of days ago, at least I think it was two days ago.
What happened prior to feeling faint today?
She walked from Southside and looked very unsteady. She had to sit down outside before we called 911.
Any pain today, Jane?
No pain at all. I just feel weak and confused sometimes.
Your skin is hot, dry, and has its normal color. We don't see any rashes or lesions either.
Your head and neck are fine with no signs of trauma. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
I've placed a 16G IV in your right arm to give fluids. Denying pain at this time as per PT.
Earlier you didn't know what year it was. Are you clearer now?
I'm with you now, but still slightly confused at times.
You mentioned being outside; were you involved in any trauma or injuries today?
No, I just felt weak and sat down. No trauma occurred as far as I know.
Any issues with your nose or mouth other than dryness from the heat?.
No. I just feel hot and a little dehydrated.
Breathe in slowly so I can listen to your lungs.
Lungs are clear. Breathing is rapid but not in distress. No audible adventitious sounds detected.
Any pain or tenderness in your stomach, or have you had nausea or bowel issues today?
No. It feels soft and normal. I haven't had any nausea or vomiting today either.
Any pain or discomfort in your legs or back during this time?.
PT is moving all her limbs. Peripheral pulses are strong in all extremities as of now.
We've attached four leads to monitor her cardiac rhythm continuously now.
Twelve-lead EKG has notably showed sinus tachycardia with nonspecific T wave abnormalities currently.
IV fluids of normal saline are now running at 500 CCs in her right arm through a 16G IV placed in the RAC
We have her on a nasal cannula at 2 liters per minute to assist with oxygen saturation monitoring.
Capnography is active for continuous breathing assessment during transport and support.
Given the symptoms and vitals, we suspect heatstroke and are following Protocol 5-2 for heat-related conditions..
Current vital signs after reassessment include BP of 148 over 78 and heart rate of 124 now.
Respiratory rate is 15 breaths per minute and SpO2 is 96% now. Glucose was undetectable yet again.
EKG reveals continued sinus tachycardia without new rhythm changes observed at this stage.
Now, her heart rate is 114, respiratory rate has increased to 42 with SpO2 at 92% on the latest measurements.
Current BP is back to 154/85, still no glucose readings obtained on the last check.
We are transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for continued care and close monitoring by hospital staff.
That makes sense to me. I'll go with [COMPANY_NAME] for next steps.
We'll assist in moving you to the stretcher now using a safe and secure method recommended for unstable patients.
Her stretcher is now secured with all straps in place for a safe and smooth transportation for [COMPANY_NAME].
She is already stable enough for transport with all medical monitoring tools active during ride to the ER.
I'll pass her care to a registered nurse at [COMPANY_NAME] with a full briefing provided before disengaging from scene.
She's on an IV drip, EKG, and capnography ready for immediate disposition arrival at emergency room in due.
Patient stays with continuous monitoring, stable for transport with maintainable vitals en route to [COMPANY_NAME] Healthcare.
16G IV in the right arm continues to function without issues. Good placement on initial insertions.
Her orientation is still a bit confused. Does she know where she is now and why she's here for transport?
I think I'm in [CITY], on the way to an ER. I feel a little disoriented still but not worse.
Does her right arm show full strength, or is movement limited or fatigued?.
Gross motor and sensory evaluation shows PM/PMS intact in all extremities per EMT partner assessment on backboard.
Her GCS is 14 with eyes open spontaneously, responding to verbal cues, and following commands - no stroke symptoms visually observed.
Earlier, she was speaking unclearly and had difficulty communicating normally as witnessed by onlookers at the scene.
Is there any discomfort in any part of her body as per your current status or otherwise she is fine now?.
Negative. I'm not in pain and haven't been all day, just a brief episode of confusion at the scene followed by fatigue.
All safety straps are active, and the stretcher is prepared for transport to the facility now.
Pre-arranged with proper facility; next step is loading into the ambulance with continuous capnography active as we head out.
She is secured, her IV and EKG leads are taped in place, and we will be ready to begin transport to Cold.
Patient will be handed off to a registered nurse upon arrival at the hospital with ongoing treatments continuing uninterrupted.
Her EKG remains on sinus tach, IV fluid is active, and she is on nasal cannula with focused monitoring still running on her before transport starts.
Transport en route is proceeding safely with patient stable, protocols followed fully as described with emergency medical conditions covered properly.
Your skin does not show signs of trauma, lacerations, or abrasions consistent with an accidental fall or stumble anywhere today.
I did not experience any trauma, falls, or injuries. Just the episode of dizziness and disorientation which didn't cause any other injuries as per my recollection.
Her transport will be made with safety straps, temperature monitored, and IV drip flowing en route to [COMPANY_NAME] admission.
Vulnerable with these symptoms, but stable for transport. Critical care is at [COMPANY_NAME] for any unfolding needs if any as per the plan.
No surpassing immediate needs identified during route. [COMPANY_NAME] is our closest facility of choice for protocol continuation.
I have already agreed, and I feel safe being transported to the next location; I am confident with the process for better care.
All charges stay at 0 outside of additional procurement needs for paramedic team during final delivery stage onboard ambualnce.
Alright, we are ready to begin the transport process with full monitoring active to the next hospital care facility listed.
